item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was reading the 2017 book by Marina J. Lostetter titled "Noumenon" at the same time I was reading this skip, and I noticed that both literature had elements of Dyson structures and unknown entities shutting off devices that came too close.

Could you two be… the same person?

item.title: Re: +200
item.content:encoded:
Thanks! I just got done reading your 001 proposal, and that was excellent right there.

I've been away from the site for a while but I'd love to come back to it as soon as I come up with a good new idea.
Well deserved. This is some great stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damnit, Foundation, this is why we can't have nice things. You see a bright shiny cloud of solar panels and then you go and give FTL tech to hyperadvanced aliens! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I initially thought this to be an artist's lazy jumping on the "it's aliens" theory for the star, but it actually developed all in an instant, very effectively too.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A wonderful look into some more space related skips, which I am always a huge fan of. I'm honored that you thought my idea of temporal sinks were good enough to use in your own work, and I'm excited to see where else they turn up!

Great stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Commenting on this to clear stuff up for future readers.

First of all, like GeometryPrime mentioned, any kind of FTL has to be loophole based, because FTL is not physically possible, ever. So, take what I'm about to say with a grain of salt.

I think the difficulty in understanding how time sinks work comes from the fact that there are multiple time streams going on. Let's break them down.

GeometryPrime more or less gets it right when they point out that the fundamental idea behind time sinks is that you speed up time for the observers from Earth, while holding it constant elsewhere. One sink is activated when the ship is launched, creating a bubble in which time is hella fast, allowing the ship to travel to destinations millions of light years away in a human-level scale.

However, the answer as to how exactly the Foundation ensures that the ship itself doesn't get destroyed from simple fatigue lies in a second T-sink, onboard every Peregrine ship. This T-sink puts the ship itself in another modified time stream, that holds time in stasis-nothing changes, ever. Fatigue, decay, and creep stress don't occur if no time passes, allowing the Foundation to not have to worry so much about how to keep an experimental ship intact millions of miles away from home. Think of this as a bubble within a bubble. The outer bubble itself is traveling at (what we perceive to be) relativistic speeds, allowing for insanely fast travel at appreciable fractions of c. However, the bubble inside that bubble is held at 0 relative to the outer bubble, not our timeline.

As a result, time inside the innermost bubble does not move, whereas the outermost bubble is moving at "stupid fast speeds." This is kind of confusing, I'm aware, and I left it vague on purpose in 3200 to allow for some more exploration in other works.

Of course, this brings up the obvious questions of "If you're fucking with time like this, why not just make it instantaneous travel?" and "Why do they even need to be in cryo freeze anyway?"

Both of those are answered by the same thing. Time sinks can't go from "normal time" to "no time" or "fast time" all at once. Much like braking a car, it takes time (HAH) for them to fire properly, and those speeding up and slowing down times are very, very long by our standards. Think of it like a plane taking off and descending-both of those processes take a fair amount of time relative to the length of the trip. Time sinks are similar in that way.

Hope this helps!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A calamaty of some sort is mentioned in other alien-related stuff like SCP-1980 (alien invasion) and 1050(obelisk of warning). Related? I mean, space is space, even if it is huge, it's still a single dimension.

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: timer expired, edits made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

This mentions "Temporal Sinks" being used to transportation, similar to that of SCP-3200. However, I propose a link to SCP-1780, considering that the Temporal Anomalies Department built these Temporal Sinks in the first place. To this end, I also propose linking SCP-2000 (since it uses Temporal Sinks in its operation) and Iteration 0 (since it focuses on Thaddeus Xyank, the head of the Temporal Department).

This also mentions "telekill", so I propose a link to SCP-148 for that reason.

Thirdly, I propose a link to Project Heimdall, considering that this skip deals with aliens that may/may not interact with Earth in the future, and Project Heimdall is the Foundation division deals with aliens.

Editing this:

The journey of nearly 1300 ly was to be undertaken through the use of Temporal Sinks

To this:

The journey of nearly 1300 ly was to be undertaken through the use of Temporal Sinks1

Editing this:

Working on it. Not like I've just got a bunch of telekill on this ship with me.

To this:

Working on it. Not like I've just got a bunch of telekill on this ship with me.

Editing this:

Farewell. Our two species may subsequent rejoin.

To this:

Farewell. Our two species may subsequent rejoin.

Footnotes
1. For more information about the design and function of Xyank/Anastasakos Constant Temporal Sinks, please contact Dr. Thaddeus Xyank or another member of the Foundation Temporal Anomalies Division.

item.title: This really Neat
item.content:encoded: I thought this was really awesome. By the way, the calamity thing you stated. You think you might make SCP-001 take on it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the sci-fi elements in this. Temporal sinks as a means of FTL hadn't really crossed my mind till now. The whole concept makes me giddy. +1

item.title: Neutralized?
item.content:encoded: These guys still exist, we don't know where they are but they know where we are, and they can show up here anytime they like. Surely they're a much bigger threat to normalcy and much harder to contain after the Peregrine mission?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The rest of the article questions the aliens' motives a couple of times so it isn't completely out of the blue.

But I agree it feels kinda hammy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My only complaint is that you randomly threw in a detail about a "calamity" at the end for seemingly no reason, which many other skips do as well. Other than that, pretty solid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just learned about Tabby's star today!

I like the idea that they thought the alien race was attacking them, but in actuality they accidentally saved an otherwise doomed species. Maybe that's not how you intended it, but I read it that way and liked it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… even if I have a hard time believing that the Foundation developed faster-than-light travel technology before a vastly more advanced alien civilization did.

Not as improbable as you might think. The ancient Greeks developed a primitive steam engine. They used it to make their temples seem even more miraculous. They also developed a simple analog computer - which was apparently used only to calculate tides, although the Foundation does contain an anomalous version of the Antikythera Mechanism. For that matter, the Inca developed wheels - which were used exclusively for children's toys, never applied to problems of transportation.

How a technology develops depends largely on how people look at things.

item.title: Re: Not Anomalous
item.content:encoded: The same phenomenon has been noted in other fictions. For instance, there's the Amalgamated Regional Militia, or ARM, in Niven's future history; they're the police arm of the United Nations, and (among other things) they monitor technological advances to make sure nothing that can disrupt the global balance is released. They have a point - in a world of eighteen billion, it's pretty easy to accidentally gum up the works - but at one point, the viewpoint character of the ARM stories wonders to himself if they go overboard in shutting down even useful advances because of perceived danger. (The invention that caused this line of thought was one that was developed to stop fires by remotely suppressing the physical reactions that make fires burn. Turned out that it could also be used to remotely suppress the reactions that keep a human alive…)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Calamity called to my mind Niven's Known Space stories - in which the Milky Way is in the process of becoming a Seyfert galaxy, one whose core has exploded in a series of massive supernovae. The ensuing radiation wavefront would be more than sufficient to sterilize the remaining galaxy.

That's why the Pak began trying to colonize the outer arms (sadly for them, there's not enough thallium out here for tree-of-life root to grow properly - we know them as "yams"), that's why someone built the Ringworld (its structure stops 40% of neutrinos, which is definitely weird), and that's why the Puppeteer Fleet of Worlds is headed for the Greater Magellanic Cloud (sane puppeteers regard hyperspace as more dangerous than five planets in a Kemplerer rosette flying at sublight velocities through interstellar space).

I imagine the Calamity is something similar. Good thing the Foundation's got an FTL system, because when that particular XK-class scenario plays out escape is the only option.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
light from the ship would travel at the same speed as the gravitational waves…

I'm gonna be totally honest here and admit that I didn't think of that. Makes me feel kinda dumb, actually - of course light travels at the speed of light.

Although as I addressed above, messing too hard with trying to make the relativity work at this point would break more things than it fixed. I'm gonna leave it, but I'm still kinda mad I made that mistake.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Perez: Gravity's still there. Just hasn't caught up with us yet - speed of light delay. Give it a few minutes. Maybe we can figure out what happened.

expository dialogue like this, i know, is for the readers to understand what's happening… but an astronaut would never explain something this basic in a matter-of-factly way to another astronaut. it just sounds silly.

also how could they know the gravity is still there if it has not yet reached them. also how could they know what happened before the gravity reached them… light from the ship would travel at the same speed as the gravitational waves…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I would assume so - plus I imagine the normal rules of time dilation still apply - moving at a high enough percentage of c will get you anywhere in any small amount of local time.

The T-Sinks perhaps don't alter the internal flow of time, but the external flow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Inside the ship, you're still experiencing interstellar travel at sublight, so it will take centuries for the crew to get there. Hence putting them in cryo."

Centuries? The target star system is 1280 light years away. If the crew experiences interstellar travel at sublight then it would take at least a few millennia to get there. And the Peregrine-9 mission travelled 535 million light years! Does the Foundation really have advanced enough technology to keep the crew alive and the ship functional for that long?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pretty much any FTL tech will have to negate time dilation in order to be practical - and really, in order to be FTL.

Otherwise, yeah. Time dilation sorta breaks and can start spitting out imaginary numbers/division by zero.

FTL tech *has* to be based on a loophole or it won't work.

From how I wrote it though, and how I understand SCP-3200, Temporal Sinks cause time within the ship, including the ship, to be much slower, so it can burn as usual but on the outside it seems to be accelerating stupid fast. Inside the ship, you're still experiencing interstellar travel at sublight, so it will take centuries for the crew to get there. Hence putting them in cryo.

On the outside though you're watching the ship zip off into the distance because of the difference in time rate. Works great for both ways of FTL travel. It just isn't in accordance with relativity - or the way time actually works - at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Frankly they don't make all that much sense for one-way FTL tech either.

I dunno. There is a realationship between speed and time dilation after all, and the math starts spitting out answers that results in you travelling backwards in time if you somehow brute-forced your way into achiveing FTL speeds.

So if the Time-Sink somehow takes advantages of those principles/mechanics then it could certainly be used as a FTL method. Weird as fuck, but then again, everything is when you start abusing physics to that level.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I also want to repeat my comment I left on 3200 that temporal sinks make no sense as a two-way FTL tech.

Frankly they don't make all that much sense for one-way FTL tech either.

Something could probably be done where time within a localized area, like a long cylinder going the whole distance, is accelerated and the ship moves through that, but honestly the cylinder would still probably have to propagate at C, and that isn't at all how temporal sinks are described in 3200.

That said, I like the foundationey feel of FTL-by-time-manipulation, so I used it anyway. Plus it has the benefit of having been used elsewhere - so in that way I see FTL T-Sinks as being sorta like SRAs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought you meant SCP-1584 and got very confused!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is really good, even if I have a hard time believing that the Foundation developed faster-than-light travel technology before a vastly more advanced alien civilization did. I also want to repeat my comment I left on 3200 that temporal sinks make no sense as a two-way FTL tech. Nonetheless, upvoted.

Also my headcanon is that the aliens are fleeing from SCP-1548 and there is nothing anyone can say to change my mind!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm sorry Prime.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very nice! It's well-written and I like the story.

One comment I would make is that I think the two footnotes should be removed: I enjoyed wondering at the very beginning whether a Dyson swarm was indeed in play and having it immediately put in a footnote slightly spoiled that.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Huh, didn't know that. Adding the images now.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Those are good, feel free to add them back in. Not everything on Wikimedia Commons is complient, no.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
I thought everything on wikimedia commons was under CC.

Here's the links.

https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:KIC_8462852_in_IR_and_UV.png

https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Class_C_Stellar_Engine.JPG

Yep, both are under CC.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Have you got links to the pages the images are from rather than to the images directly? I need to be able to check the license info.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
First image:

https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/5/5e/KIC_8462852_in_IR_and_UV.png

Second Image:

https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/6/64/Class_C_Stellar_Engine.JPG

I stated that they were from Wikimedia Commons. Was that not in-depth enough?

Anyway, these links work. Am I free to re-add the images?

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Images used in posted articles must comply with the site's image policy. A source must be provided on the discussion page and the source must be compatible with the site's license. The author will be notified after this posting via Wikidot PM and will have 48 hours to properly source the image before it is edited out of the page.

EDIT: As no source was linked within the time limit, the images have been removed and the author notified. They are free to add them back if a complient source can be found.

item.title: Re: Not Anomalous
item.content:encoded: I think in this particular case it worth containing cause the knowledge of some strange Calamity, which scare the hell out of them so much that: "All other species have escaped" can seriously shaiken modern society.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 2001: An SCP Odyssey

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You are welcome GeometryPrime!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks! Will fix.

Edit: I done fixed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found a spelling error! where it says furthermore, it says furtherNORE.

item.title: Re: Not Anomalous
item.content:encoded:
In this universe I fall more on the side of the Serpent's Hand - I kinda do see the Foundation as not exactly a great thing. The "Jailors."

They keep hold over Keters, which is nice, but they also rob mankind of lots of awesome knowledge.

item.title: Re: Not Anomalous
item.content:encoded: Fair enough, I sometimes forget the very diverse nature of our little hobby, which is one of the stronger points. It does suggest a Foundation that is damaging to humanity, going overboard in restricting what people know. I could also honestly see it being debated internally, and now that they've started containment, they can't really stop. It's a rather well-written piece, I just felt a need to voice my issues. My initial point, that it isn't an anomaly, is really immaterial. The Foundation thought it was, and they contained it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mean, yeah, I could - but then I'd be distracted from my current SCP project. I'll probably revisit this one someday and see if I can fix it though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I noticed all of those worms right after posting. I think there's a way to make everything conform to science without changing the big ideas, but it would require a lot of changes. The article seems to have been very well received anyway, though, so ¯\_(ツ)_/¯

item.title: Re: Not Anomalous
item.content:encoded:
I mean, I guess there's not much I can do to convince you. Alien civilization is not strictly speaking anomalous - however the Foundation is concerned mainly with keeping mankind in a bubble, unaware of the goings-on outside what the average person would consider normal.

The average person would not consider aliens normal.

Therefore they are a threat to normalcy and must be contained. There are other non-anomalous alien life forms in containment - but that's ultimately irrelevant.

Excessively advanced technology is also contained on a regular basis. Stuff mankind doesn't have yet is considered anomalous and contained.

The fact is that the Foundation's interests are not necessarily aligned with your interests or even my interests as the author - and in my headcanon, the Foundation's view of "normalcy" is a narrow-minded one.

Ultimately, though, it doesn't matter because there is no canon. My Foundation would do this. Maybe yours wouldn't, and that's OK. Neither of us is wrong - both of our headcanons are correct from our own points of view.

item.title: Re: Not Anomalous
item.content:encoded:
A star screaming threats at us from across space and time is an anomaly. A planet that somehow evolved human life and is populated by parasitic insects ripped straight from hypno-erotica is anomalous. An alien that hasn't aged in xxxx years and possess a device that contravenes standard physics to provide life support is anomalous. All of these things are a threat to normalcy, something that utterly challenges our understanding and that we are in no way prepared to handle. Alien civilization is not anomalous and is not a threat to normalcy. It's presence has been predicted by mathematical models in the mainstream, supressing evidence makes as much sense as suppressing evidence of gravitational waves, quarks, or the higgs-boson.

If anything, suppressing information is a greater threat to normalcy. People are going to figure it out eventually, and when they do, they'll wonder who was trying so hard to cover it up. People will start digging, and the Foundation will have to contend with the Streisand Effect. By covering up something they dont need to they create an unnecessary risk of discovery and broken masquerade.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Shit. You've got me there.

Eh, whatever. I'm leaving it because plugging that opens a whole new can of continuity worms that I'd rather not open.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! Will fix.

item.title: Re: Not Anomalous
item.content:encoded:
Any alien intelligence is considered to be a threat to normalcy as the Foundation sees it, and is therefore considered anomalous despite breaking no physical laws.

An object visible from earth that threatens to break the masquerade is a significant risk to the Foundation, and therefore more must be lerned about it to determine the best course of action.

item.title: Not Anomalous
item.content:encoded: The Dyson swarm array is not inherently anomalous, nor is star lifting. This is theoretically possible under normal physical models, and is detectable through non-anomalous means. The Foundation has no reason to get involved in any way, as this falls entirely outside their purview and interests. A non-anomalous, non-threatening, non-harmful megastructure being used to leave our galaxy is a waste of resources compared to any given keter or most Euclids. At most, the Foundation would take a wait and see approach to determine if anything about it was actually anomalous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Small error:
The line:

This reflector is aligned such that only the darkened side is visible from Earth, but at no point obscures KIC 8462852.

Repeated twice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And upvoted. That works for me :)

Final question: you mention that the system is no longer perceivable to astronomers on Earth, because it was TS'd away, but wouldn't that information take 1300 years to reach the Earth?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll see what I can do - because you're exactly right.

I'll make a few changes here and there, see if I can fix it.

EDIT: Fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see you've already made a comparison to 2317. One of the main reasons that article is so damn effective is that it withholds the big scare until the very end, in a way that this article does not. More specifically, it bothers me that you mention the "Calamity" as early as you do. If you didn't introduce it at all at the beginning, the addenda would be more effective. You could vaguely hint at it just a little more throughout the mission, to build tension - then drop the wham line.

Changing that would elevate the article from a B+ to an A for me. The mission dialogue is very well done, and your technical writing is understandable without being reductive.

Novote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am not allowed to give critique until I make my own SCP that isn't bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can you put your finger on just what seems off about it? I'm not 100% sure I understand.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm… This SCP seems off. I am not saying it is bad, but it just seems off.

Maybe SCP-3029 has a connection with The Destroyer?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You don't exactly need to have the collapsible as Level 4 clearance to keep the twist. You could have the collapsible say "SCP-3029 - UPDATED (date)".

As far as the rest of this goes, the hard sci-fi is fun but the SCP ends too quick. You set up an intriguing mystery and a nice atmosphere during the mission, but by the time it's getting good it stops. The "calamity" is also too vague to give any sense of dread, so the ending ends up being fairly weak.

Novoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe you're right - but while that isn't 100% right in-universe, it serves a purpose in the piece. That is, to make the twist work - it's important to the pacing that the reader doesn't find out the skip is gone until they open the collapsible.

Admittedly that isn't the most amazing justification, but at least there's a little bit of precedent there - SCP-2317 has a similar bit in its final iteration.

Did I pull it off as well as in that one? No way. But there is a certain logic to that decision.

EDIT: I did the thing 9Volt suggested below.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're welcome.

Also, HD 189733b and such skip surrounding KIC 8462852 sounds more similar than the exoplanet itself.

Well, atleast KIC 8462852 doesn't rains glass. :D
